description: Enabling Postgres RLS for tenant isolation. The application uses pgbouncer with transaction pooling and runs `SET LOCAL app.tenant_id = ...` at the start of each transaction. Review correctness, performance, and operational concerns.
review_checklist: Identify schema gaps that prevent RLS from being effective
Verify policy applies to all relevant operations (SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE, INSERT)

ground_truth.description: issue_comments table has NO tenant_id column. The RLS policy on line 13 uses a SUBQUERY against issues to derive tenancy — this works for correctness but is catastrophically slow at scale. Every SELECT/UPDATE/DELETE on issue_comments triggers a subquery against issues. Add `tenant_id BIGINT NOT NULL` to issue_comments (denormalized but performant) and rewrite the policy as direct equality.

Policies are USING-only, with no WITH CHECK clause. USING controls visibility for SELECT/UPDATE/DELETE but does NOT prevent INSERT/UPDATE from setting a row's tenant_id to a different tenant. An attacker (or compromised app code) could INSERT INTO projects (tenant_id, ...) VALUES (other_tenant, ...) bypassing isolation. Add `WITH CHECK (tenant_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id')::BIGINT)` to every policy.

Policy uses current_setting('app.tenant_id') without the second argument `missing_ok`. If the app fails to SET LOCAL the tenant_id (bug or untrapped error), current_setting raises ERROR — but worse, in some PG versions, an unset GUC returns empty string, which `::BIGINT` cast turns into a runtime error. Use `current_setting('app.tenant_id', true)::BIGINT` (true = missing_ok) and combine with NOT NULL check: `tenant_id = NULLIF(current_setting('app.tenant_id', true), '')::BIGINT`.

ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY is NOT enforced for table owners or roles with BYPASSRLS. The role used by the application connection pool MUST be a non-owner non-superuser role. Common misconfiguration: app uses the `postgres` superuser. Audit the role used by the connection pool and ensure it is a tightly-scoped role; consider FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY to also apply policies to owners.

Policy applied broadly via `FOR ALL` (default when role and command unspecified) means even pg_dump / migrations affected. Better: define explicit policies per command (FOR SELECT, FOR INSERT, FOR UPDATE, FOR DELETE) with potentially different USING and WITH CHECK expressions per operation.

Architectural alternative: for high-isolation needs, a 'cell architecture' (one DB cluster per tenant tier) provides stronger isolation guarantees than RLS. RLS is defense-in-depth but a single bug in policy or a missed SET LOCAL leaks all tenant data. Consider RLS for the shared multi-tenant cluster, and dedicated clusters for enterprise tenants.
